ASSESSMENT/PLAN:  ***** ***** is a 48 y.o. female with a recent diagnosis of right breast IDC which is ER weak positive (2%), PR low positive (7%), ***** neg (IHC 2+, FISH 2.March 09.8=1.4) and ***** 36% with ***** and axillary LN involvement here in consultation with medical oncology to discuss neoadjuvant chemotherapy.      First, we reviewed her pathology and imaging results. She has weakly ER and PR positive, ***** negative disease. She has locally advanced disease with *****-proven LN involvement, but no evidence of distant metastatic disease on PET/CT.     For local control, she will need lumpectomy or mastectomy - we defer to our surgical colleagues about which is the best option for her. She will likely also need post-operative radiation.     We explained that for systemic treatment, we would recommend neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to surgery given that she has ER and PR low positive disease with LN involvement. We discussed the advantages of offering chemotherapy in the neoadjuvant setting in order to assess response to treatment, and to increase the chances of breast conserving surgery. Outside the context of a clinical trial, we would recommend AC/T. We typically give the taxol first, followed by four cycles of TC. We then explained the option of clinical trial enrollment with the ISPY clinical trial. We explained that some of these arms involve getting taxol with an experimental therapy. We discussed the role of the ***** result as a screening for the ISPY 2 clinical trial. We explained that in the context of this clinical trial, patients receive paclitaxel and or an experimental regimen first, and then proceed with the Adriamycin and Cytoxan. At present, HER-2/neu negative patients are randomized to receive one of the 
 following regimens:  1. Control arm: Taxol weekly for 12 weeks, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   2. Taxol weekly + pembrolizumab (an immunotherapy/checkpoint inhibitor) every 3 weeks for 12 weeks, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   3. ***** every 3 weeks for x 12 weeks (not with taxol), followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   4. Taxol weekly with oral olaparib (a PARP inhibitor) and durvalumab (an immunotherapy/checkpoint inhibitor) for 12 weeks, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   5. Taxol weekly for *****-***** (a *****-9 agonist) with weekly Taxol and every 3 week Pembrolizumab for 12 weeks, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   6. Taxol weekly with ***** (an immunotherapy/checkpoint inhibitor) every 3 weeks for 12 weeks, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   7.Taxol weekly with ***** (an immunotherapy/checkpoint inhibitor) every 3 weeks and ***** 3767 (***** antibody) for 12 weeks, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles   8. ***** 985 (trastuzumab *****) - ***** ***** targeting antibody-drug conjugate for ***** low, followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles    9. Oral paclitaxel plus ***** (an oral PGP inhibitor) plus ***** (an immunotherapy/checkpoint inhibitor) with or without carboplatin followed  AC every three weeks for 4 cycles.     In order to participate in the trial, patients must undergo a baseline core biopsy for research purposes and a baseline protocol specific MRI. During the trial, patients will undergo one additional core biopsy of the breast mass in order to assess the biological changes that have occurred in the cancer in response to the ***** therapy plus chemotherapy. Patients will also undergo additional MRIs during the course of neoadjuvant chemotherapy to assist in assessing response to treatment. We discussed the very ***** trial design and explained that if the patient is randomized to the non-paclitaxel 
 containing arm (such as ***** or *****), they can switch back to paclitaxel if not responding or receive it post operatively. In any of the arms, if the patient appears to have progression of her tumor either by physical exam or breast imaging, there is the option of discontinuing the study treatment and either moving on to the AC or switching to paclitaxel.     We reviewed the anticipated side effects and toxicities of chemotherapy including increased risk of infection, hair loss, fatigue, and possibility of ovarian failure. We explained that anthracyclines are associated with a small chance of causing permanent damage to the heart (estimated to be about 1% with 4 cycles of adriamycin and cyclophosphamide) and that ***** are associated with development of neuropathy which is most often transient and rarely permanent.    We also mentioned that given she was ER/PR low positive, we would still recommend 5-10 years of endocrine therapy after surgery. We did not discuss the options in length today, but can do so at a later date.    She asked about whether she could continue to work as an ICU nurse during chemo. We advised her that many patients choose to work during weekly taxol, but it will largely depend on which arm she is randomized to, as some of the experimental arms are likely to be associated with more toxicity. Most patients choose to take off time during AC and then after recovering from chemotherapy.     Plan:  -- ***** order TTE  -- Port placement  -- Chemotherapy teaching session  -- She is interested in screening for ISPY; then will need research core biopsy, research breast MRI, labs. F/u pending *****.   -- F/u results of genetic testing (done locally last Mon)  -- After surgery, she will be considered for post operative radiation and we will recommend 5-10yrs of adjuvant endocrine therapy     RTC in 1-2 weeks in person to start chemo    Patient seen and discussed with breast oncology attending 
 Dr. ***** *****.     ***** *****, MD PGY-5  Hematology/Oncology Fellow

Reason_for_Visit.summary: A 48-year-old female with a recent diagnosis of right breast IDC, which is ER weak positive (2%), PR low positive (7%), HER2 negative (IHC 2+, FISH negative), and has axillary LN involvement. The purpose of this visit is to discuss neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

Cancer_Diagnosis.Type_of_Cancer: ER weak positive (2%), PR low positive (7%), HER2 neg (IHC 2+, FISH 2.March 09.8=1.4) invasive ductal carcinoma with high-grade DCIS component
Cancer_Diagnosis.Stage_of_Cancer: Locally advanced with axillary LN involvement, no evidence of distant metastatic disease

Clinical_Findings.findings: Right breast IDC with a 2.6 x 1.9 x 2.3 cm oval mass, anterior depth, with no skin or nipple invasion. Multiple prominent right level 1 axillary lymph nodes, one with a biopsy clip compatible with known LN micrometastasis. MRI shows an enlarged level [REDACTED] lymph node. PET/CT shows a hypermetabolic right breast nodule with a peak SUV of  5.1, no hypermetabolic lymph nodes, and no evidence of metastatic disease. Pathology: IDC, grade 2 or 3, with a high-grade DCIS component, ER 2% weak positive, PR 7% low positive, [REDACTED] 2+, FISH negative, [REDACTED] 36%. Right axillary LN with a 0.2mm focus of micrometastatic carcinoma, no extracapsular extension. Physical exam: No palpable masses, no skin changes, no lymphadenopathy noted.

Response_Assessment.response_assessment: On treatment; response assessment not available from current visit.

Medication_Plan.medication_plan: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy with options including AC/T (doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide followed by paclitaxel) or enrollment in the ISPY clinical trial with various regimens such as Taxol weekly for 12 weeks followed by AC every three weeks for 4 cycles, or Taxol with pembrolizumab, olaparib, durvalumab, or other experimental therapies. Post-operative adjuvant endocrine therapy for 5-10 years will be recommended after surgery.

Therapy_plan.therapy_plan: Port placement, chemotherapy teaching session, will start neoadjuvant chemotherapy with options including AC/T or ISPY clinical trial enrollment. After surgery, will consider post-operative radiation and recommend 5-10 years of adjuvant endocrine therapy.

64.letter:
Dear Patient,
You recently visited us for a consultation about your breast cancer.
Your cancer is a type called invasive ductal carcinoma, which means it started in the milk ducts.
The cancer is weakly positive for estrogen (ER) and low positive for progesterone (PR), and does not have extra HER2 protein.
The cancer is at a locally advanced stage, meaning it has spread to the lymph nodes in your armpit but has not spread to other parts of your body.
Your imaging tests show a 2.6 x 1.9 x 2.3 cm mass in your right breast, with no skin or nipple invasion. There are also some enlarged lymph nodes in your armpit.
We will be starting you on neoadjuvant chemotherapy, which is treatment given before surgery to shrink the cancer.
You will have a port placed to make it easier to give you the chemotherapy.
We will also teach you about the chemotherapy and start the treatment in 1-2 weeks.
After surgery, you will likely need radiation therapy to the breast area.
You will also need 5-10 years of endocrine therapy after surgery to help prevent the cancer from coming back.
We will also be doing a research core biopsy and a research MRI of your breast.
We are waiting for the results of your genetic testing, which was done last Monday.
We understand this is a difficult time, and our team is here to support you every step of the way.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Reason_for_Visit.summary: 52 year old with newly diagnosed stage IB right breast cancer with mixed lobular and ductal features, 4.3 cm tumor, ER positive, PR positive, HER2 negative. S/p right lumpectomy with sentinel node biopsy. Initial consult for discussion of adjuvant therapy options including radiation and hormonal blockade.

Cancer_Diagnosis.Type_of_Cancer: ER+/PR+/HER2- mixed ductal and lobular carcinoma

Clinical_Findings.findings: Patient has a 4.3 cm mixed ductal and lobular carcinoma in the right breast with negative margins, 11/24 lymph nodes involved, ER+, PR+, HER2-. No new imaging findings noted. Physical exam shows healing left lumpectomy site with no mass in the left breast, no palpable masses, skin changes, or lymphadenopathy.

Response_Assessment.response_assessment: On treatment; response assessment not available from current visit.

Medication_Plan.medication_plan: Continue current medications including acetaminophen 1000 mg by mouth every 8 hours, ibuprofen 600 mg by mouth every 8 hours as needed for pain or temperature, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth daily before lunch, lisinopril 40 mg by mouth daily before lunch, metformin 500 mg by mouth daily before lunch. Continue testing blood sugar daily as directed. No new medications or changes to current medications were discussed.

Therapy_plan.therapy_plan: We discussed the role of chemotherapy to decrease the risk of systemic recurrence. Due to her low risk, I do not recommend chemotherapy. We discussed the role of radiation to decrease the risk of local recurrence. Plan to proceed with [REDACTED] +/- bone medication after radiation is completed.

radiotherapy_plan.radiotherapy_plan: We discussed the role of radiation to decrease the risk of local recurrence. She has an appointment with Dr. [REDACTED] tomorrow.

Imaging_Plan.imaging_plan: DEXA to assess bone health before the next visit with me.

follow_up_next_visit.Next clinic visit: RTC after radiation. Plan to proceed with [REDACTED] +/- bone medication after radiation is completed.

81.letter:
Dear Patient,
You visited us for a new patient evaluation about your breast cancer.
Your cancer is in the right breast and is a type that grows in response to hormones (estrogen) but does not have extra HER2 protein.
The cancer is at an early stage and has not spread to other parts of your body.
We talked about treatments to lower the risk of the cancer coming back.
We decided not to use chemotherapy because your risk is low.
You will have a DEXA scan to check your bone health before your next visit.
We will start a medication to help prevent the cancer from coming back after radiation.
We also referred you for exercise counseling to help with your health.
We understand this is a difficult time, and our team is here to support you every step of the way.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Patient Name: ***** *****  ***** *****: 11/10/20    HPI: ***** ***** is a 60 y.o. female with a CHEK2 mutation, multiple sclerosis, and metastatic breast cancer with disease in bone, soft tissue, liver, and possibly meninges who is seeking recommendations for ongoing management and treatment. She is a patient of Dr. ***** at ***** in ***** *****.    Please see detailed oncologic history below in the problem list, summarized from ***** records and patient report.     ONCOLOGIC HISTORY:  Patient Active Problem List    Diagnosis Date Noted    Metastatic breast cancer (CMS code) 11/09/2020     Right breast cancer:  1999: S/p lumpectomy and ALND (no report)  Adjuvant radiation per patient report  No adjuvant treatment    Second primary right breast cancer:  2006: 2.0cm grade 2 IDC ER+ (50%), PR+ (95%), *****-  10/11/05: S/p right simple mastectomy with negative margins. Due to prior ALND in ***** ***** ***** ***** ***** ***** this surgery.  03/01/06: CAF x 6 completed  *****/*****-*****/*****: Five years tamoxifen  11/02/12: ***** left hip (prophylaxis for *****)?  07/09/19: Screening mammogram negative    Metastatic disease:  11/05/19: Presented to the ED with increasing back and neck pain  11/05/19: CT C-spine: There is a pathologic fracture through the base of the C2 vertebral body. There is retropulsion of fragment with narrowing of the spinal canal.  11/05/19: CT CAP: Pathologically enlarged right axillary and mediastinal lymphadenopathy, worrisome for metastatic disease. Extensive lytic/blastic osseous lesions scattered throughout the visualized axial and appendicular ***** with sternal body, multiple 
 vertebral body, right humeral neck, and left inferior pubic ramus/acetabular pathologic fracture deformities.  11/05/19: MRI total spine: C2 pathologic burst fracture with fragment retropulsion resulting in moderate spinal canal stenosis. Diffuse osseous metastatic disease. No definite lesions at imminent risk of additional fracture.  11/05/19: NM bone scan: Diffuse foci of uptake involving the calvarium, ribs, spine, pelvis, and femurs c/w osseous metastatic disease.  11/07/19: Axillary LN biopsy: Metastatic carcinoma, ER+ (71-80%), PR- (<1%), *****- (IHC 2+, FISH 3.June 04.9=1.3)  ***** testing from ***** biopsy from November 2019: *****+, ***** mutation  11/07/19: Started zolendronic acid. 4 mg monthly x 3 then every 3 months  *****/*****-*****/*****/*****: Radiation C1-3, T12-L2, 5 fractures ***** total  11/21/19: MRI brain: Demyelinating disease in the supratentorial and infratentorial brain, without active disease  12/03/19-early July 2020: Letrozole and Palbociclib 125mg daily ***** d, 7 d off. Stopped in early July due to *****  12/17/19: MRI total spine: Multifocal enhancing marrow replacement in the clivus, vertebral bodies and posterior elements, bilateral sacral ala and iliac bones, likely from diffuse osseous metastatic disease. Unchanged compression fracture of C2, expanding the anterior and posterior cortices, with mild spinal canal narrowing. Compression fractures involving the superior endplates of L1 and L3, with approximately 25% loss of height, again noted, with increased enhancing STIR hyperintensity in T12 through L2, likely from with suspected evolving compression fracture of L2, without significant retropulsion at this time.  03/11/20: CT CAP: Enlarged right axillary and mediastinal lymphadenopathy suspicious for metastatic disease although, the lymph nodes appear to be slightly decreased in size from the prior exam. Diffuse osseous metastatic disease unchanged from the prior exam  07/01/20: CT CAP: Pathologic right axillary mediastinal 
 lymphadenopathy, worrisome for nodal metastatic disease, stable since 03/10/2020. Multiple hypoattenuating lesions scattered throughout the right and left hepatic lobes, worrisome for metastatic disease, increased in size and in number compared to 03/10/2020. Extensive blastic osseous metastatic disease involving the visualized portions of the axial/appendicular ***** with lower lumbar spine, left inferior pubic ramus, probable left acetabular, sternal, and multiple rib pathologic fracture deformities, unchanged since 03/10/2020. Small left pleural effusion with associated compressive atelectasis and/or consolidation.  08/06/20: Screening mammogram: Negative  Early August 2020: Started on capecitabine one week one, one week off. Patient accidentally took only 500mg BID until mid-September. We had a lengthy discussion with the patient and her daughter which we reviewed her breast cancer diagnosis, workup and treatment history to date. I reviewed all of the relevant and available clinic notes, imaging, and pathology reports. First, we reviewed *****'s pathology confirming that she has metastatic breast cancer which is estrogen receptor positive 
 and ***** negative based on core needle biopsy of an axillary LN in November 2019. She presented with extensive lytic bone lesions at that time. She has since developed metastatic disease to the liver and pachymeninges and possibly to the leptomeninges, although her first CSF cytology was negative for malignant cells. We explained that metastatic breast cancer is not curable, but it is treatable and there are therapies that will extend the quality and duration of life.     We reviewed her treatment course to date. She was initially started on letrozole/palbociclib in December 2019, but this was discontinued due to PD to the liver and LN in July 2020. WE assured ***** that this was an appropriate treatment choice as initial therapy of *****+/*****- metastatic disease. Upon progression, she was switched to Xeloda (initially 500mg BID inadvertantly, then 1000mg BID in mid-September, and 1500 mg BID since late September).     She recently had brain MRIs on 09/29/20 and again on 11/07/20, which showed diffuse irregular ***** enhancement along the bilateral cerebral convexities and right petroclival region as well as *****'s ***** The more recent scan showed possibly worsening of disease in the internal auditory canal, which would be consistent with her recent right hearing loss.  We tried to explain to the patient and family the difference between ***** based/***** disease and leptomeningeal disease and stressed that it can be very difficulty radiographically to distinguish the two. While her first CSF cytology was negative for malignant cells, her radiographic and clinical findings raise suspicion for leptomeningeal disease.    Recently, her neurologic symptoms are getting progressively worse, with no improvement in her vision and now auditory symptoms. Despite her initial CSF cytology being negative, we explained that  false negatives are not uncommon and we recommend repeat LP with cytology now. We also recommend referral to 
 radiation oncology for consideration of radiation therapy (either just to her internal auditory canal or to a larger portion of her *****). We would also consider starting low dose steroids to see if this improves her symptoms.     In terms of future options, if a CT CAP shows clear progression on Xeloda, we would consider another hormone-based therapy strategy. We would consider fulvestrant plus the ***** inhibitor ***** (*****) (***** ***** 2019), given her known ***** mutation on Strata testing. In this study, in the cohort of patients with *****-mutated cancer, progression-free survival at a median follow-up of 20 months was 11.0 months in the *****-fulvestrant group, as compared with 5.7 months in the placebo-fulvestrant group. However, this regimen does not have great CNS penetration, but there are few regimens that do.    Given that she has metastatic disease to the bone, we agree with continuing zolendronic acid    Recommendations:  -- Repeat CT CAP now  -- Repeat LP for CSF cytology  -- Repeat MRI spine to r/o LMD in the spine  -- Recommend referral to radiation oncology to consider either focal CNS radiation or possible even *****. The patient is interested in a UCSF Rad Onc opinion as well.  -- Could consider starting low dose steroids to see if reduction of swelling improves any of her symptoms  -- Continue xeloda 1500mg BID for now  -- At time of progression of disease, could consider fulvestrant + ***** given the ***** mutation on STRATA testing  -- Continue zolendronic acid    Patient seen and discussed with breast oncology attending *****. ***** *****.    ***** *****, MD PGY-5  Hematology/Oncology Fellow

83.letter:
Dear Patient,
You recently had a televisit with us to discuss your ongoing treatment for metastatic breast cancer.
Your cancer is ER+ (grows in response to hormones like estrogen) and PR- (does not respond to progesterone) and does not have extra HER2 protein.
The cancer has spread to your bones, soft tissue, liver, and possibly the meninges (the protective layers covering the brain).
Your blood tests show a low platelet count (80) and a slightly low hemoglobin (8.4), but other values are within normal range.
Recent imaging shows that the cancer has grown in your liver and possibly in the protective layers of your brain.
We recently increased your dose of capecitabine (Xeloda) to .
The goal of your treatment is to manage your symptoms and improve your quality of life.
We will continue your current medication, Xeloda, at the higher dose. We may also consider starting you on low-dose steroids to help with swelling.
We recommend you see a radiation oncologist to discuss possible radiation treatments for your brain.
We will repeat a lumbar puncture (LP) to check your cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) for cancer cells.
We will also repeat a CT scan of your chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and an MRI of your spine to check for any new cancer growth.
Your blood tests are mostly normal, so no new lab tests are planned at this time.
We understand this is a difficult time, and our team is here to support you every step of the way.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

There was no active enhancing lesion. CT CAP on 07/01/2020 showed multiple hypoattenuating lesions in the right and left hepatic lobes, increased in size and number compared to 03/10/2020. Extensive osseous metastatic disease involving the axial/appendicular skeleton with old lower lumbar spine and left inferior pubic ramus pathologic fracture deformities. Physical exam showed right eye droop, otherwise no significant changes. No new palpable masses, skin changes, or lymphadenopathy noted.

findings: brain MRIs on 09/29/20 and again on 11/07/20, which showed diffuse irregular [REDACTED] enhancement along the bilateral cerebral convexities and right petroclival region

sentences.text: We recently increased your dose of capecitabine (Xeloda) to 1500mg twice a day.

extraction_values.recent_changes: Increased capecitabine dose to 1500mg BID in late September.

recent_changes: Xeloda (initially 500mg BID inadvertantly, then 1000mg BID in mid-September, and 1500 mg BID since late September).

sentences.text: The goal of your treatment is to manage your symptoms and improve your quality of life.

goals_of_treatment: metastatic breast cancer is not curable, but it is treatable and there are therapies that will extend the quality and duration of life.

sentences.text: We will continue your current medication, Xeloda, at the higher dose. We may also consider starting you on low-dose steroids to help with swelling.

sentences.text: We recommend you see a radiation oncologist to discuss possible radiation treatments for your brain.

extraction_values.radiotherapy_plan: Recommend referral to radiation oncology to consider either focal CNS radiation or possible even [REDACTED]. The patient is interested in a UCSF Rad Onc opinion as well.

radiotherapy_plan: Recommend referral to radiation oncology to consider either focal CNS radiation or possible even [REDACTED].

sentences.text: We will repeat a lumbar puncture (LP) to check your cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) for cancer cells.

Reason_for_Visit.summary: 36 year old with a Stage III left sided breast cancer diagnosed in March 2019, now with metastatic disease to the brain. The purpose of this visit is a new patient evaluation for ongoing management of metastatic breast cancer.

Cancer_Diagnosis.Type_of_Cancer: ER+/PR+/HER2- invasive ductal carcinoma, metastatic biopsy ER-/PR-/HER2-
Cancer_Diagnosis.Stage_of_Cancer: Originally Stage IIIB, now metastatic (Stage IV)
Cancer_Diagnosis.Metastasis: Yes (to brain, lungs, and lymph nodes)
Cancer_Diagnosis.Distant Metastasis: Yes, to brain, lungs, and lymph nodes

Clinical_Findings.findings: Patient has a history of Stage III left-sided breast cancer with two tumors measuring 4.0 and 2.6 cm, with 23 lymph nodes involved. The primary tumor was poorly differentiated with histologic grade III/III. The patient has metastatic disease to the brain, lungs, and lymph nodes. She had resection of one brain metastasis and stereotactic XRT to both. Imaging shows lung and lymph node metastases. The patient is currently on Xeloda and has no palpable masses, skin changes, or lymphadenopathy on physical exam. No new imaging findings are reported in this visit.

Medication_Plan.medication_plan: She is on xeloda. If her 2 positive disease is confirmed, she will need anti her 2 therapy. Restaging after 3 months of therapy if she is stable clinically. If she has progression on xeloda, consider clinical trials using immunotherapy or using immunotherapy with chemotherapy off trial.

Therapy_plan.therapy_plan: She is on xeloda and I would recommend restaging after 3 months of therapy is she is stable clinically. We discussed clinical trials using immunotherapy or using immunotherapy with chemotherapy off trial if she has progression on xeloda.

radiotherapy_plan.radiotherapy_plan: She had resection of one brain metastasis and stereotactic XRT to both brain metastases.

Procedure_Plan.procedure_plan: Planned procedures include hormone studies and HER2 testing on the residual disease and metastatic foci. Receptors on the breast residual disease and metastatic foci will be repeated by her primary oncologist.

Genetic_Testing_Plan.genetic_testing_plan: I recommending doing her 2 on the brain metastasis and hormone studies and her 2 on the residual disease. I discussed her case and asked her primary oncologist to repeat receptors on her breast residual disease and also the her 2 on the metastatic foci.

87.letter:
Dear Patient,
You visited us for a new patient evaluation about your breast cancer.
Your cancer is ER+/PR+/HER2- invasive ductal carcinoma, which means it grows in response to hormones (estrogen) but does not have extra HER2 protein.
The cancer is now at an advanced stage and has spread to your brain, lungs, and lymph nodes.
You are currently taking a medication called Xeloda.
We will check the receptors on your breast cancer and the metastatic foci to see if you need additional treatments.
If your cancer continues to be stable after three months of treatment, we will do more tests to see how it is responding.
If the cancer starts to grow again, we will consider clinical trials or other treatments that use immunotherapy.
We understand this is a difficult time, and our team is here to support you every step of the way.
You will follow up with me as needed or if the cancer starts to grow again.
You are a full code, which means you want all possible treatments if you become very ill.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Clinical_Findings.findings: Patient is currently on cycle 2 of AC treatment for breast cancer. Most recent cycle was cycle 3, completed 2 weeks ago. Pathology from initial imaging showed a 3 cm tumor. After treatment, residual IDC of 2.2 cm with approximately 60% cellularity was noted. Physical exam via video observation shows slight tissue swelling in the axilla, no erythema or drainage, palpitations, and port site tenderness. Gastrointestinal symptoms include constipation and mild nausea. Thyroid function tests show elevated TSH and normal Free T4, suggestive of autoimmune thyroiditis leading to hypothyroidism. No significant imaging findings are mentioned in the note.

Treatment_Changes.recent_changes: GCSF dose reduced to 50%, will add PO dex at home prn N/V, will add olanzapine 10 mg prn N/V, switch to granisetron for anti-nausea

Response_Assessment.response_assessment: Patient is currently on cycle 2 of AC treatment for breast cancer. Most recent cycle was cycle 3, completed 2 weeks ago. Pathology from initial imaging showed a  3 cm tumor. After treatment, residual IDC of 2.2 cm with approximately  60% cellularity was noted. No specific imaging or tumor marker evidence of response is mentioned in the note.

Medication_Plan.medication_plan: Continue cycle 4 of AC in 1 week, reduce GCSF dose to 50%. Add PO dex at home prn N/V, add olanzapine 10 mg prn N/V. Try granisetron for N/V.

Therapy_plan.therapy_plan: Continue with cycle 4 of AC in 1 week (dose delay x 1 wk) to facilitate recovery from sx's.

89.letter:
Dear Patient,
You visited us for a follow-up about your breast cancer.
Your cancer is in the right breast and is at an early to locally advanced stage.
We are using a treatment called AC to try to cure your cancer.
Your blood tests show that your thyroid is not working as well as it should, but other tests are mostly normal.
You are currently on cycle 2 of AC treatment.
We have made some changes to your treatment to help with side effects.
We will continue with cycle 4 of AC in about a week to give you time to recover.
We will also reduce the dose of a medication called GCSF to 50% and add other medications to help with nausea.
We will see you again after you finish radiation therapy, which should be in about 1 to 1.5 months.
We understand this is a difficult time, and our team is here to support you every step of the way.
Sincerely, Your Care Team

Lab_Results.lab_summary: Alanine transaminase 27, Aspartate transaminase 60, Alkaline Phosphatase 67, Bilirubin, Direct 0.1, Bilirubin, Total 0.4, Sodium 140, Potassium  4.4, Chloride 108, CO2 26, BUN 20, Creatinine 0.59, Glucose 101 (from 04/12/2011), WBC Count 4.1, RBC Count  4.50, Hemoglobin 12.4, Hematocrit 37.5, MCV 84, Platelet Count 215, Neutrophil Absolute Count 2.46, Lymphocyte Absolute Count 1.16

Clinical_Findings.findings: MRI pelvis: Moderate interval increase in size and extent of right iliac lesion abutting the right acetabular articular surface and extending along the right pubic root. Multiple new suspicious T2 hyperintense/T1 hypointense lesions within the sacrum, left ischial tuberosity, and left femur. Left femoral head metastatic focus appears unchanged. Interval increase in bilateral hip effusions. PET/CT: Confirms increase in bone metastases and new soft tissue stranding in right perirenal area. Physical exam: +1cm right iliac LN, soft, mobile, RLE non-pitting, dependent edema.

Treatment_Changes.recent_changes: Started lasix 10mg once a day with KCL 10Meq once daily.

Response_Assessment.response_assessment: MRI pelvis and PET/CT scans show an increase in bone metastases and new lesions, indicating that the cancer is not responding to the current treatment regimen of everolimus, exemestane, and denosumab.

Medication_Plan.medication_plan: Continue lasix 10mg daily with KCL 10Meq daily, continue denosumab, topical antifungal 2 times daily

Therapy_plan.therapy_plan: Continue exemestane daily, continue lasix 10mg daily with KCL 10Meq daily, continue denosumab, topical antifungal 2 times daily, PET/CT next week to evaluate disease burden, labs monthly, RTC in 1 month.

90.letter:
Dear Patient,
You came to us for a follow-up visit about your breast cancer.
Your cancer is at an advanced stage and has spread to your bones.
The cancer grows in response to hormones (estrogen) but does not have extra HER2 protein.
Your blood tests are mostly normal.
Recent imaging shows that the cancer has grown in your bones and new spots have appeared.
You are taking everolimus and exemestane to manage your cancer.
We have started you on lasix to help with swelling and potassium to keep your electrolytes balanced.
The goal of your treatment is to control the cancer and manage symptoms.
We will continue lasix and potassium, and you will keep taking denosumab to strengthen your bones.
Next week, you will have a PET/CT scan to see how the cancer is doing.
We will check your blood tests every month to monitor your health.
You will come back to see us in one month to review your labs and the results of your scan.
Sincerely, Your Care Team
